title: श्री शिव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शिव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

ॐ शिवो महेश्वरः शम्भुः पिनाकी शशिशेखरः।
वामदेवो विरूपाक्षः कपर्दी नीललोहितः॥ १॥

शङ्करः शूलपाणिश्च भवो भर्गो दिगम्बरः।
द्वादशैतानि नामानि शिवस्य परमात्मनः॥ २॥

प्रातरुत्थाय यो भक्त्या शिवनामानि यः पठेत्।
सर्वपापविनिर्मुक्तः शिवलोकमवाप्नुयात्॥ ३॥

नमोऽस्तु शम्भवे नित्यं नमस्ते गिरिजापते।
नमोऽस्तु भीमनाथाय नमस्ते कृत्तिवाससे॥ ४॥

नमो गङ्गाधराय च नमस्ते त्रिपुरान्तक।
नमोऽस्तु वृषभेशाय नमस्ते विषमेक्षण॥ ५॥

नमो नमः कालकालाय नमस्ते रुद्ररूपिणे।
नमो नटेश्वराय च सदा पञ्चमुखाय ते॥ ६॥

इत्येतत् द्वादशनामानि त्रिसन्ध्यं यः पठेन्नरः।
गोघ्नश्च सुरापश्च चोरश्च गुरुतल्पगः॥ ७॥

सर्वपापैर्विनिर्मुक्तः शिवसायुज्यमाप्नुयात्।
एतच्छिवस्य द्वादशनाम संस्मरन् सदा॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री शिव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shiva Dvadash Nama Stotram ||

Om Shivo Maheshvarah Shambhuh Pinaki Shashishekharah.
Vamadevo Virupakshah Kapardi Nilalohitah. || 1 ||

Shankarah Shulapanishcha Bhavo Bhargo Digambarah.
Dvadasaitani Namani Shivasya Paramatmanah. || 2 ||

Pratarutthaya Yo Bhaktya Shivanamani Yah Pathet.
Sarvapapavinirmuktah Shivalokamavapnuyat. || 3 ||

Namostu Shambhave Nityam Namaste Girijaapate.
Namostu Bhimanathaya Namaste Krittivasase. || 4 ||

Namo Gangadharaya Cha Namaste Tripurantaka.
Namostu Vrishabeshaya Namaste Vishamekshana. || 5 ||

Namo Namah Kalakalaya Namaste Rudrarupine.
Namo Nateshvaraya Cha Sada Panchamukhaya Te. || 6 ||

Ityetat Dvadashanamani Trisandhyam Yah Pathennarah.
Goghnacha Suraapashcha Choraashcha Gurutalpagah. || 7 ||

SarvapapaVinirmuktah Shivasayujyamapnuyat.
Etachchhivasya Dvadashanama Samsmaran Sada. || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Shiva Dvadash Nama Stotram Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning: शिव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्र में भगवान शिव के बारह पवित्र नाम हैं: शिव, महेश्वर, शम्भु, पिनाकी, शशिशेखर, वामदेव, विरूपाक्ष, कपर्दी, नीललोहित, शंकर, शूलपाणि, और भव। जो भक्त प्रातःकाल उठकर इन नामों का पाठ करता है वह सर्वपापों से मुक्त होकर शिवलोक प्राप्त करता है। फिर शम्भु, गिरिजापति, भीमनाथ, कृत्तिवासी, गंगाधर, त्रिपुरान्तक, वृषभेश, विषमेक्षण, कालकाल, रुद्र, नटेश्वर और पञ्चमुख को नमन करने से सर्वपापमुक्ति और शिव सायुज्य प्राप्त होता है।
benefits: Chanting 12 sacred names of Lord Shiva destroys all sins
Morning recitation grants liberation and Shiva Loka
Purifies even the gravest of sinners
Three-time daily recitation leads to union with Shiva
Invokes the blessing of divine protection and spiritual merit
description: Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram (श्री शिव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise dedicated to Lord Shiva. Attributed to Shiva Purana, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to three-time daily recitation leads to union with Shiva, bestow divine protection and spiritual merit, and purify even the gravest of sinners. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Shravan month. The text contains 8 verses and takes around 5 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram - 12 sacred names of Lord Shiva in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Powerful prayer for sin removal and moksha.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram and which 12 names does it contain?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram (श्री शिव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) is a sacred Sanskrit hymn that enumerates and praises twelve principal names (dvadasha-nama) of Lord Shiva, the Paramatman. The first sloka lists the twelve names: 'Shivo Maheshvarah Shambhuh Pinaki Shashi-shekharah, Vamadevo Virupakshah Kapardi Nila-lohitah, Shankarah Shula-pani-shcha Bhavo' — namely (1) Shiva (the auspicious), (2) Maheshvara (the great Lord), (3) Shambhu (the source of bliss), (4) Pinaki (wielder of the Pinaka bow), (5) Shashi-shekhara (moon-crested), (6) Vamadeva (the beautiful god), (7) Virupaksha (three-eyed), (8) Kapardi (the matted-haired), (9) Nila-lohita (blue-throated red-bodied), (10) Shankara (giver of peace), (11) Shula-pani (trident-bearer), and (12) Bhava (the source of becoming). The stotram is preserved in the Shiva Purana tradition. (Sources: Shiva Purana dvadasha-nama section; classical Shaiva stotra tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the phala of reciting the Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The stotram itself contains an extraordinary phalashruti that is one of the most sweeping in the entire Shaiva corpus. Verse 3 declares: 'Pratar-utthaya yo bhaktya Shiva-namani yah pathet, sarva-papa-vinirmuktah Shiva-lokam-avapnuyat' — whoever rises at dawn and devotedly recites these names of Shiva is freed from all sins and attains Shivaloka. Verses 7 and 8 go further: even one who has committed the gravest sins listed in dharmashastra — go-ghna (cow-killer), sura-pa (alcohol-drinker), chora (thief) and guru-talpa-ga (one who violates the guru's bed) — if he recites these twelve names at trisandhya (the three sandhyas of dawn, noon and dusk), is liberated from all sins and attains Shiva-sayujya (union with Shiva). This radical declaration of the redemptive power of nama-smarana is characteristic of the Puranic bhakti tradition. (Sources: Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram phalashruti verses 3, 7-8; Shiva Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the names Pinaki, Kapardi and Nila-lohita?
seoFaqs.answer: These three names from the dvadasha-nama list each carry deep iconographic and Vedic meaning. Pinaki — 'wielder of the Pinaka' — refers to Shiva's great bow Pinaka, with which in the Tripura-samhara he destroyed the three flying cities of the asuras with a single arrow; the name also identifies him as the warrior-protector of dharma. Kapardi — 'the one with kaparda (matted hair)' — refers to Shiva's iconic jata-bhara, the matted dreadlocks of the maha-yogi atop which Ganga descends and the crescent moon rests; this is one of the oldest Vedic names of Rudra found in the Yajurveda Sri Rudram. Nila-lohita — 'blue (and) red' — recalls the Halahala episode from the samudra-manthana when Shiva drank the world-destroying poison and held it in his throat, which turned blue (nila-kantha), while the rest of his body retained its reddish (lohita) hue. (Sources: Sri Rudram; Shiva Purana; classical iconography.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the additional names invoked in verses 4 to 6 of the stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Verses 4 through 6 of the Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram extend the praise with another twelve invocations forming a second tier of names. Verse 4: Shambhu, Girijapati (consort of the mountain-born Parvati), Bhima-natha (lord of Bhima), Krittivasa (clad in tiger-skin). Verse 5: Gangadhara (bearer of the Ganga), Tripurantaka (destroyer of Tripura), Vrishabhesha (lord of Vrishabha — Nandi the bull), Vishameksha-na (the one with uneven eyes — three eyes including the third inner eye). Verse 6: Kala-kala (the death of death — the time beyond time), Rudra-rupi (the form of Rudra), Nateshvara (the lord of dance — Nataraja), Pancha-mukha (the five-faced — Sadyojata, Vamadeva, Aghora, Tatpurusha, Ishana). Together these expand the meditation into a fuller dhyana of Shiva's cosmic, iconographic and esoteric forms. (Sources: Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram verses 4-6; classical Shaiva iconography.)

seoFaqs.question: What is trisandhya recitation and why is it specified for this stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Trisandhya means 'the three sandhyas' — the three twilight junctions of each day: pratah-sandhya (dawn, when night meets day), madhyahna-sandhya (noon, when sun is at meridian) and sayam-sandhya (dusk, when day meets night). These three sandhya periods are considered the most powerful and pranically charged moments of the day in Vedic tradition, when the gunas shift and consciousness is naturally subtler. The classical brahmanical disciplines of sandhya-vandana, gayatri-japa and arghya-pradana are performed at these times. The Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram specifies trisandhya recitation because the gravest sins listed in verse 7 (go-ghna, sura-pa, chora, guru-talpa-ga) — sins that ordinary expiation cannot fully cleanse — require the most powerful possible recitation discipline, sustained at all three sandhyas, to attain the supreme phala of Shiva-sayujya. (Sources: classical sandhya paddhati; Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram verse 7.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram differ from the Shiva Ashtottara Shatanamavali?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are name-meditation prayers to Lord Shiva but differ in length, depth, and ritual use. The Shiva Dwadash Nama Stotram is a short composition of just 12 principal names (with 12 additional namaskars) compiled as a single stotram of 8 slokas — taking only about 5 minutes to recite — designed for daily morning recitation, trisandhya parayana and rapid sin-removal. The Shiva Ashtottara Shatanamavali is a much longer enumeration of 108 names of Shiva, used principally for archana (ritual offering of flowers, akshat or bilva-patra to a Shiva-linga or murti, with one item offered per name as 'Om Shivaya namah, Om Maheshvaraya namah' etc.) — taking about 20-30 minutes. The Shiva Sahasranama (1008 names) is the most expansive form. Tradition recommends starting with dvadasha-nama, expanding to ashtottara-shatanamavali, and finally to sahasranama. (Sources: classical Shaiva archana paddhati.)
